messages: Big update: I just received a notice from the registrar — the exam date was changed unexpectedly to earlier than I thought, and it will now take place in person under strict no-electronics rules.
This throws my month-long plan off completely. I have much less time now and can't rely on digital notes in the exam room. How should I re-prioritize?
Given the shorter window and the exam being paper-based on site, should I focus more on memorization drills or on timed problem practice?
My main materials are a mix of PDFs and online slide decks. Since I can't use devices at the venue, what's the fastest way to make reliable offline study aids?
Realistically, with days rather than weeks left, what topics get the most attention? Are there classic high-yield areas that tend to show up in this course?
What cram-friendly memory techniques work when you have to retain formulas and answer structure quickly? I'm thinking mnemonics, but anything better?
Does it still make sense to do full mock exams under these circumstances, or should I switch to shorter, focused drills for weak topics?
Logistics question: since the test center bans electronics, are there standard accepted stationary items I should double-check? Pens, ID, calculator rules?
How should I adjust sleep and caffeine in the remaining days so I'm sharp on test day without burning out?
I also have joint study partners who planned to share digital summaries; what's a quick way to convert collaborative resources into tangible paper packets we can use?

Concerning my daily rhythm now: I can fit in two substantial study blocks and a couple of short reviews; mornings are my best time. Past exams are printed but most of my notes live online. With the time compressed and devices not allowed in the testing space, how would you divide those blocks?
If there were only three actions I could reliably do each day that would raise my score the most, what should they be? Prioritize by impact.
As the assessment approaches, what must-have physical revision items should I prepare for quick consults during breaks — single-sheet key points, formula lists, answer templates, or something else?

Could you map out a tactical, day-by-day plan for the remainder that emphasizes paper-based practice, highest-yield content, and adequate sleep? Keep it realistic for someone doing long concentrated blocks.
For turning online outlines into one-page printed aids, what's the most effective way to structure those pages for rapid recall under pressure?

Finally, please produce a concise, prioritized schedule for the remaining time that assumes no digital aids will be usable during the test, tells me exactly what to print and memorize, explains how to practice past papers with pen and paper, and finishes with a simple morning-of checklist. Make it directly actionable for a very compressed prep window.
